old_claim.note: Mass Production provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Mass production scaled the supply of standardized consumer goods that postwar consumerism encouraged households to acquire.

source_shape.source_locator: Suburbanization and The Organization Man sections: OpenStax describes annual passenger-car production jumping from 2.2 million to 8 million between 1946 and 1955 and purchases of appliances, furniture, home decorations, cars, vacations, and other consumer goods spurring industries.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: OpenStax documents large postwar production and household purchases of cars, appliances, furnishings, vacations, and other consumer goods.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports mass production as the supply-scaling side of consumerism, not a single required technical method for every consumer purchase.

ontology_before: Consumerism treated mass production as a generic enabling prerequisite.
ontology_after: Consumerism treats mass production as the commercial scaling substrate that made abundant standardized consumer goods available.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: consumerism -> mass_production is now a commercial_or_scaling_dependency edge.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to consumer-protection activism rather than high-consumption social order.
A stronger source shows postwar consumerism did not depend on abundant standardized goods.
The mass_production node is narrowed away from standardized high-volume consumer-goods production.
short_reason: Mass production scales the consumer-goods supply that consumerism consumes.
